- audit: Verify a spec unit's implementation against its authoritative spec. Reads the manifest, reads spec files, walks target code, catalogs every gap, and writes task files for each finding.
- spec: Read the implementation spec for a given I/O module. Usage: /spec <module>. Modules: console, process, designer, graphics, display-elements, shapes, opc
- design-qa: Inventory all implementations of a cross-cutting feature across the codebase, surface inconsistencies, ask targeted design questions, and write the answers as an authoritative decision file. Run this BEFORE /audit on any feature marked ⚠️ NOT SPECCED in the manifest.
- spec-index: Print a quick-reference index of every audit unit — unit ID, canonical name, wave, and current task status. Use this to look up the correct unit ID or module name before running spec-scout or feature-agent.
- agent-guide: Print a reference guide for all custom agents in this project — what each one does, when to use it, how to launch it, and usage examples.
- wrapup: Close the current work unit (summary + review + doc updates)
- review: Run a verification review of work done since the last [initprompt]
- deepreview: Run a deep verification review including full file contents
- summarize: Generate an LLM-based summary of the current work unit
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Code updates related to mouse mode:

## Claude Code NO_FLICKER Mode with Mouse Support

Claude Code recently released a NO_FLICKER mode that solves terminal flickering issues and brings full mouse interaction support. This mode was introduced in version 2.1.88 and is currently in the experimental phase.

### Key Features:
- Native mouse support allows clicking directly on the input box to position the cursor and enables clicking to expand/collapse tool call results or open URLs in the browser
- NO_FLICKER mode uses a backup screen buffer rendering system, similar to vim or htop

### Enabling Mouse Mode:
Users can add `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1` to their shell configuration file (such as ~/.zshrc) or to Claude Code's settings.json.

### Mouse Mode in Terminal Multiplexers:
There is also a known issue: When running Claude Code inside terminal multiplexers like zellij that have their own mouse mode enabled, clicking generates garbage characters because both Claude Code and the multiplexer enable conflicting mouse tracking escape sequences. A config option like `--no-mouse` or `"mouse": false in settings` has been requested to disable mouse tracking in Claude Code's TUI.

This is about feature compatibility, not a security "hijack" — it's a normal technical issue that occurs when multiple terminal applications enable mouse tracking simultaneously.

Here are the search results for Claude Code CLI mouse capture, SSH, PuTTY, terminal mouse reporting, and ANSI escape codes:

**Key Findings:**

1. **Focus Reporting Escape Sequences in Claude Code**: When using Claude Code in certain terminals, focus reporting escape sequences [I and [O are being injected into the input interface when clicking with the mouse or using modifier keys, and these escape sequences should be filtered by Claude Code's input handler.

2. **Kitty Keyboard Protocol Issues**: CSI u escape sequences from the kitty keyboard protocol can appear in Claude Code input, and Claude Code should ignore/filter these key events. Notably, the same issue does NOT occur when using PuTTY to SSH into the same machine and running Claude Code CLI.

3. **ANSI Escape Code Handling**: When a Bash tool call captures large output containing ANSI escape sequences (such as colored log output), the raw escape sequences are stored in the context window without stripping.

4. **Terminal Mouse Reporting**: Terminal applications can enable focus reporting mode (ANSI mode 1004) which causes the terminal to send focus-related escape sequences.

The search results show several GitHub issues related to Claude Code's handling of various terminal escape sequences, particularly around mouse input, keyboard protocols, and ANSI color codes across different terminal environments and SSH configurations.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about PuTTY terminal mouse reporting and application capturing of mouse clicks via SSH:

## Mouse Reporting in PuTTY

PuTTY allows the server to send control codes that let it take over the mouse and use it for purposes other than copy and paste. Applications which use this feature include the text-mode web browser links, the Usenet newsreader trn version 4, and the file manager mc (Midnight Commander).

## How It Works When Enabled

It's possible for the server to ask to handle mouse clicks in the PuTTY window itself. If this happens, the mouse pointer will turn into an arrow, and using the mouse to copy and paste will only work if you hold down Shift.

## Configuration

Details of this feature and how to configure it can be found in section 4.6.2 and section 4.11.2 of the PuTTY documentation. This allows you to control whether xterm-style mouse reporting is enabled or disabled for the terminal applications running over your SSH connection.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about disabling xterm mouse reporting in PuTTY:

## How to Disable Xterm-Style Mouse Reporting

PuTTY allows the server to send control codes that let it take over the mouse and use it for purposes other than copy and paste. Applications which use this feature include the text-mode web browser links, the Usenet newsreader trn version 4, and the file manager mc (Midnight Commander). When running one of these applications, pressing the mouse buttons no longer performs copy and paste.

To disable this feature, you can disable it using the 'Disable xterm-style mouse reporting' control. With this box ticked, the mouse will always do copy and paste in the normal way.

## Where to Find This Setting

You can disable this in Settings > Terminal > Features > disable xterm-style mouse reporting, OR you can change how PuTTY presents itself to the server altogether (ie. VT220).

This setting is available in PuTTY's configuration dialog under the Terminal Features section, and once enabled, it will prevent applications from taking over mouse control for their own purposes, allowing normal copy and paste functionality to continue working.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Code's NO_FLICKER mode and mouse tracking:

## Overview
Fullscreen rendering is an alternative rendering path for the Claude Code CLI that eliminates flicker, keeps memory usage flat in long conversations, and adds mouse support.

## Mouse Tracking Enabled by Default
Mouse support ships baked into no flicker mode by default, and applications like Vim, htop, and less enable mouse mode when they switch to alternate buffer, and Claude Code follows the same pattern.

## Version Requirements
Fullscreen rendering is an opt-in research preview and requires Claude Code v2.1.89 or later. You can enable it with the /tui fullscreen command in your current conversation, or set CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1 on versions before v2.1.110.

## Key Features
Some upsides include no more flickering, no more jumping, constant memory and CPU usage as the conversation grows, and mouse support. Users can click to move their cursor within the input box, and some other UI elements are also clickable.

Note: Claude Code No Flicker Mode currently operates as a research preview feature, and mouse capture can be disabled through configuration flags while still keeping flicker-free rendering active.

content.text:
Here's the full picture:

## Root cause

A recent Claude Code update introduced **fullscreen/NO_FLICKER rendering mode** (v2.1.89+). This renderer, modeled on how vim/htop work, sends xterm mouse-reporting escape codes (`ESC[?1000h` and friends) to the terminal at startup to enable mouse support. PuTTY faithfully honors those codes — so the moment Claude Code sends them, PuTTY hands all mouse events to the app instead of doing local copy/paste. Your mouse pointer turns into an arrow and right-click stops working normally.

It wasn't your config that changed. Claude Code's startup sequence started emitting mouse-enable codes it didn't emit before.

---

## Fixes — pick one

**Option 1 — Disable mouse tracking in Claude Code (recommended)**

Add to `~/.claude/settings.json`:
```json
{
  "mouse": false
}
```
Or set the env var before launching:
```bash
CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=0 claude
```
Or in your shell profile:
```bash
export CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=0
```

**Option 2 — Block it on the PuTTY side**

In your saved PuTTY session: **Terminal → Features → tick "Disable xterm-style mouse reporting"**. This makes PuTTY ignore any app's request to take over the mouse. You lose nothing — Claude Code doesn't depend on actual mouse events to function.

**Option 3 — Hold Shift to bypass (workaround, not a fix)**

When mouse reporting is active in PuTTY, holding **Shift** while clicking/dragging restores normal copy/paste behavior. Useful if you want to keep Claude Code's mouse support for other things.

---

## Why it's specifically a PuTTY issue

Other terminals (Windows Terminal, iTerm2, etc.) often have their own mouse-event handling layer that absorbs or negotiates these codes differently. PuTTY passes xterm mouse reporting through faithfully by design, which is correct behavior — it just means you feel the full effect of an app enabling mouse mode.

---

- [No-flicker mode causes mouse scroll input to be interpreted as text · Issue #42297](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/42297)

- [NO_FLICKER mode announcement (Threads)](https://www.threads.com/@boris_cherny/post/DWmbMxDk_WA/video-today-were-excited-to-announce-no-flicker-mode-for-claude-code-in-the-terminal)
- [Focus reporting escape sequences issue · Issue #10375 · anthropics/claude-code](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/10375)
